findings.title: Images not pinned to immutable digest
findings.description: Both services use floating major.minor tags (`mongo:8.0`, `redis:7.4`). Docker resolves these tags at pull time, meaning a silent `docker pull` on the same tag can introduce a different image layer if the upstream publisher pushes a new patch build under the same tag. The compose file offers no reproducibility guarantee between developer machines or between pull dates.
findings.impact: Developers on different machines or after a Docker Hub push may run different underlying image versions. A malicious or buggy upstream patch could be pulled transparently without any visible change to the compose file.
findings.recommendation:
Pin images to their SHA-256 digest for full reproducibility:
```yaml
image: mongo:8.0@sha256:<digest>
image: redis:7.4@sha256:<digest>
```
Obtain digests with `docker pull mongo:8.0 && docker inspect --format='{{index .RepoDigests 0}}' mongo:8.0`. Alternatively, accept the floating-tag risk explicitly as a documented team decision if the overhead of maintaining digests is judged too high for local dev.

findings.title: No production-scope warning comment in compose file
findings.description: The file contains no comment, label, or warning indicating it is for local development only and must not be used in production. Without authentication configured on either MongoDB or Redis, accidental use of this file on a network-accessible host (e.g., a cloud VM where 127.0.0.1 binds only to loopback but the developer forgets to check) exposes unauthenticated database endpoints.
findings.impact: An engineer who copies or references this compose file for a staging or production deployment would launch MongoDB and Redis with no authentication. The 127.0.0.1 compensating control is host-specific and would not protect a cloud VM with public IPs.
findings.recommendation:
Add a header comment to the file:
```yaml
# LOCAL DEVELOPMENT ONLY — no authentication configured.
# Do NOT use in staging or production. Ports are bound to 127.0.0.1
# as the sole security compensating control (Decision 9).
```

findings.title: No container resource limits defined
findings.description: Neither the mongodb nor the redis service defines memory or CPU limits (`mem_limit`, `cpus`). On a developer machine, a memory leak or runaway query in either container can consume all available RAM and cause the host OS to OOM-kill other processes.
findings.impact: Degraded developer experience if containers consume excessive resources; other local services (IDE, browser) may become unresponsive. Low probability in practice for vanilla MongoDB and Redis with no load, but still a missing defensive boundary.
findings.recommendation:
Consider adding soft limits appropriate for local dev, for example:
```yaml
deploy:
  resources:
    limits:
      memory: 512m
```
This is a quality-of-life improvement, not a hard security requirement for the stated scope.
